blocks.content:
You now have numbers from k6, probe metrics, logs, and traces for the same incident window. Fill the tables below, then paste one short paragraph for stakeholders if you need a narrative.

To complete the postmortem, complete the following steps.

1. Copy the postmortem table into your notes.

1. Fill every **Your value** cell using the scratch pad and earlier milestones.

| Signal | Your value |
| --- | --- |
| k6 window | Date/time UTC; VUs peaked to 100 |
| k6 result | Thresholds pass/fail; checks pass/fail; p95 ___ ms; trend (one phrase) |
| Probe metrics | Peak region; peak latency ___ s; slowest recovery; vs k6 p95 (rough multiple) |
| Logs | ~___ lines; dominant message pattern |
| Traces | Hot child span; ~___% of trace duration |

   Optional one-paragraph narrative: On [window], k6 [pass/fail] with p95 [___] ms; probes in [region] peaked at [___] s; logs showed [pattern]; traces pointed to [span] at [___]% of the trace.

1. Answer the reflection prompts in the table.

| Reflection | Your answer (1–2 sentences) |
| --- | --- |
| Does the lab's lenient p95 threshold reflect production risk? What would you set instead? | |
| What failure mode might need 30–60 minutes of sustained load to appear? | |
| If a probe stayed high 15 minutes after VUs returned to zero, what does that suggest? | |
| How would you change `stages` for a gradual ramp (soak) instead of a spike? | |
| One k6 metric and one log line to justify go/no-go? | |

Check your work: you can move on when both tables are filled with real numbers from your run. If a row is empty, return to that milestone, widen the **Explore** window if needed, then open troubleshooting for that step.

In the next milestone you reach the path conclusion and optional follow-on learning paths.
